original_abstract:
Clouds play a critical role in reshaping the composition of atmospheric aerosols by acting as a reactive medium where gas- and particulate-phase substances interact. To explore the molecular makeup of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) and the effects of cloud processing, we collected two samples of supercooled cloud water at the Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.) near Steamboat Springs, Colorado. Using reversed-phase solid-phase extraction and ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, we identified approximately 3,000 distinct molecular formulas in each sample. 

The detected species spanned molecular weights up to 700 Da and fell into several elemental categories: oxygen-only (CHO), nitrogen-containing (CHNO), and sulfur-containing (CHOS and CHNOS) compounds. The average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio was approximately 0.6, suggesting a more oxidized state than typically found in water-soluble organic carbon from aerosol studies—a shift likely driven by aqueous-phase oxidation within the clouds. 

Our analysis reveals that the AOM is heavily influenced by biogenic secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and residential wood combustion. Notably, 60% of the CHO formulas in the cloud water were identical to those found in laboratory SOA generated from the ozonolysis of monoterpenes like α-pinene and d-limonene. CHNO compounds were the most abundant and frequent species, linked to NOx-influenced oxidation and wood smoke. Furthermore, the nighttime sample showed a higher prevalence of multi-nitrogen formulas, pointing to the importance of nitrate radical chemistry in the dark. We also identified several sulfur species containing reduced sulfur, though their specific origins require further study. These findings provide an unambiguous molecular fingerprint of cloud water organic anions in the Rocky Mountains, offering a clearer understanding of how aqueous chemistry modifies the atmosphere.
humanized_abstract:
Clouds are a sink and flux space that continually modify the atmospheric aerosol composition through heterogeneous processes occurring within the cloud reactive environment. Much remains to be learned regarding the exact chemical makeup of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) and the mechanisms and extent to which AOM is affected by cloud processes. Two supercooled cloud water samples were collected on June 23, 2008, at Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.), in the Steamboat Springs Mountains of Colorado. We identified approximately 3000 unique molecular formulas using a reversed-phase solid-phase extraction technique and analysis with ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry.

Species with molecular weights up to 700 Da were observed and could be classified into CHO, CHNO, CHOS, and CHNOS types. An average O/C ratio of about 0.6 was derived, which is higher than that of the water-soluble organic carbon typically reported in aerosol studies. The more oxidized composition of cloud organic nitrates, as deduced from the O/C ratio, is consistent with the aqueous-phase processes occurring in clouds.

The AOM is dominated by biogenic secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and residential wood burning. We identified 60% of the CHO formulas present in cloud water aerosol as being also present in laboratory SOA produced from the ozonolysis of monoterpenes (such as α-pinene and d-limonene) that are also biogenic. The CHNO class was found to be the largest and most diverse including compounds produced during NOx-dominated photochemical oxidation and those characteristic of wood smoke sources. The nighttime sample had a greater diversity of multi-nitrogen containing formulas that were attributed to nitrate radical chemistry at night. Reduced sulfur containing sulfur compounds were also found in the cloud water sample and their sources require additional investigation. In summary, this work has identified an unambiguous molecular signature of cloud water organic anions in the Rocky Mountains and quantifies the impacts of aqueous processes on the atmospheric environment.

document.output:

document.input:
Clouds play a critical role in reshaping the composition of atmospheric aerosols by acting as a reactive medium where gas- and particulate-phase substances interact. To explore the molecular makeup of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) and the effects of cloud processing, we collected two samples of supercooled cloud water at the Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.) near Steamboat Springs, Colorado. The detected species spanned molecular weights up to 700 Da and fell into several elemental categories: oxygen-only (CHO), nitrogen-containing (CHNO), and sulfur-containing (CHOS and CHNOS) compounds. The average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratio was approximately 0.6, suggesting a more oxidized state than typically found in water-soluble organic carbon from aerosol studies—a shift likely driven by aqueous-phase oxidation within the clouds. 

Our analysis reveals that the AOM is heavily influenced by biogenic secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and residential wood combustion. Notably, 60% of the CHO formulas in the cloud water were identical to those found in laboratory SOA generated from the ozonolysis of monoterpenes like α-pinene and d-limonene. CHNO compounds were the most abundant and frequent species, linked to NOx-influenced oxidation and wood smoke. Furthermore, the nighttime sample showed a higher prevalence of multi-nitrogen formulas, pointing to the importance of nitrate radical chemistry in the dark. We also identified several sulfur species containing reduced sulfur, though their specific origins require further study. These findings provide an unambiguous molecular fingerprint of cloud water organic anions in the Rocky Mountains, offering a clearer understanding of how aqueous chemistry modifies the atmosphere.
